Based on the search results, here's what I found about cross-platform IDE plugin frameworks with unified APIs:

## Agent Client Protocol (ACP)

The most significant development in this space is the **Agent Client Protocol (ACP)**, which is emerging as a unified standard for AI coding agents across multiple IDEs.

### Key Details:

JetBrains is collaborating with Zed on the Agent Client Protocol (ACP) – an open protocol that lets AI coding agents work inside editors, including JetBrains IDEs, if both are ACP-compatible.(no content)The idea is that coding agents that support ACP will get automatic integration with editors. In the absence of a standard, developers building coding agents have to create separate add-ins for each editor they support, or rely on a command-line interface (CLI) that works in the terminal, independent of any specific editor.

### Benefits of ACP:

The protocol promises no vendor lock-in (use the agent you prefer inside the IDE you love), confidence and control (see exactly what the agent is planning, review diffs, and approve actions), and a great UX in your IDE with streaming updates and clear intent.(no content)The ACP protocol makes agents editor-agnostic, so agents work anywhere you code.

### Supported Editors:

Full ACP integration is coming soon to IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, and other JetBrains IDEs.(no content)Zed offers modern editor with integrated ACP support for seamless agent interaction. There's also an Agent-shell.el plugin that brings ACP agents to Emacs, and Neovim support through CodeCompanion & avante.nvim plugins.

### VS Code Status:

A key question is whether VS Code itself will support ACP. There is an open issue designated "under discussion" and assigned to Microsoft engineer Rob Lourens. Lourens said on GitHub that "this is interesting, but I don't plan to implement it right away."

### Other Cross-Platform Solutions:

CodeTogether is a remote pair programming tool that enables real-time collaboration across multiple IDEs and editors, including Visual Studio Code, IntelliJ IDEs and Eclipse. It allows developers to work together regardless of their preferred development environment and is IDE agnostic.

In summary, **ACP appears to be the leading unified API effort** for cross-platform IDE plugin development, particularly for AI coding agents, with JetBrains and Zed as primary collaborators. VS Code support remains uncertain at this time.

Here are the search results for JetBrains IDE plugin development SDK in 2025:

## Key Updates for 2025

### IntelliJ Platform 2025.3 Changes
The IntelliJ Platform 2025.3 release introduces a unified IntelliJ IDEA distribution while maintaining strong backward compatibility.(no content)Starting with version 2025.3, IntelliJ IDEA Community and Ultimate will be delivered as a unified platform. This marks a significant architectural change that makes plugin development and distribution easier.(no content)If you built your plugin against the IntelliJ Platform 2025.2 or earlier, it should remain compatible with 2025.3. You don't need to rush to update right away.(no content)When you're ready to target 2025.3, update to IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin 2.10.4 or later and switch to the new intellijIdea() dependency helper.

### Important Note for 2025.3
IntelliJ IDEA Community Edition (IC) builds for 2025.3 and later are no longer available. Trying to resolve artifacts using IC-2025.3, IC-253.x.y, or the intellijIdeaCommunity() dependency helper will cause an error.

## Development Tools

### IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin
JetBrains maintains the IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin, which many developers use to build, test, and deploy plugins with ease. Recently, they released version 2.x, introducing a completely reworked interface to simplify development.(no content)It provides tasks to run the IDE with your plugin and to package and publish your plugin to the JetBrains Marketplace. To make sure that a plugin is not affected by API changes, which may happen between major releases of the platform, you can quickly verify your plugin against other IDEs and releases.

### Plugin DevKit
The Plugin DevKit has been a core tool for nearly two decades—but recent enhancements make it more powerful than ever. Developers can now benefit from: In-editor documentation where you can hover over plugin XML tags and attributes to view the corresponding documentation directly inside the IDE. Improved completion and navigation: Extension points, language IDs, and Action IDs all offer rich auto-completion and go-to-declaration functionality.

### Kotlin Notebook Integration (2025.2+)
Building on the Kotlin Notebook plugin, JetBrains has started exploring how to connect it with the IntelliJ Platform. This enables the direct execution of IntelliJ Platform code from a notebook file within the active IDE runtime. The iterative workflow of Kotlin notebooks allows you to brainstorm, build, and test plugin features faster. The integration with the IntelliJ Platform makes moving the finished code into your plugin easy. This initial idea has developed into a strong integration, and as of the 2025.2 release, the functionality is now available for use.

## Getting Started

There are two main ways of creating a new Gradle-based IntelliJ Platform plugin project: a dedicated generator available in the New Project Wizard – it creates a minimal plugin project with all the required files; and the IntelliJ Platform Plugin Template available on GitHub – in addition to the required project files, it includes configuration of the GitHub Actions CI workflows.

## Community Resources
Building a strong community is just as important as improving tools for plugin development. JetBrains recently created a new home for the community by bringing the public Slack for plugin developers and the IntelliJ Platform forum into the JetBrains Platform community space.

Here are the search results for the Language Server Protocol (LSP) and universal editor plugins:

## Overview

The Language Server Protocol (LSP) defines the protocol used between an editor or IDE and a language server that provides language features like auto complete, go to definition, find all references, etc.(no content)The Language Server Protocol (LSP) is an open-standard protocol developed by Microsoft. It enables communication between development tools and Language Servers. Language Servers can provide language-specific features such as code completion, documentation, and formatting, which is far easier than implementing language support from scratch.

## The Problem LSP Solves

LSP creates the opportunity to reduce the m-times-n complexity problem of providing a high level of support for any programming language in any editor, IDE, or client endpoint to a simpler m-plus-n problem. For example, instead of the traditional practice of building a Python plugin for VSCode, a Python plugin for Sublime Text, a Python plugin for Vim, a Python plugin for Sourcegraph, and so on, for every language, LSP allows language communities to concentrate their efforts on a single, high performing language server that can provide code completion, hover tooltips, jump-to-definition, etc.(no content)The Language Server Protocol allows for decoupling language services from the editor so that the services may be contained within a general-purpose language server. Any editor can inherit sophisticated support for many different languages by making use of existing language servers.

## Key Benefits

Language Servers can be implemented in any language and run in their own process to avoid performance cost, as they communicate with the code editor through the Language Server Protocol. Furthermore, any LSP-compliant language toolings can integrate with multiple LSP-compliant code editors, and any LSP-compliant code editors can easily pick up multiple LSP-compliant language toolings. LSP is a win for both language tooling providers and code editor vendors!(no content)LSP is not restricted to programming languages. It can be used for any kind of text-based language, like specifications or domain-specific languages (DSL).

## Editor Support

- **Visual Studio Code**: VS Code provides a Node SDK to build Language Server extensions.
- **Visual Studio**: Starting with Visual Studio 2017 version 15.8, support for the common Language Server Protocol is built into Visual Studio.
- **JetBrains IDEs**: The LSP API is publicly available as part of the IntelliJ Platform in the following IDEs: IntelliJ IDEA, WebStorm, PhpStorm, PyCharm, DataSpell, RubyMine, CLion, DataGrip, GoLand, Rider, and RustRover.
- **Sublime Text**: There's an LSP plugin that aims to expose features from any LSP-based language server in a consistent manner.

## Technical Details

The Language Server Protocol (LSP) is a common protocol, in the form of JSON RPC v2.0, used to provide language service features to various code editors. Using the protocol, developers can write a single language server to provide language service features like IntelliSense, error diagnostics, find all references, and so on, to various code editors that support the LSP.(no content)The latest version of the LSP specification is version 3.17.

## Resources

- **Official LSP site**: https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/
- **Community implementations**: https://langserver.org/
- Several companies have come together to support its growth, including Codenvy, Red Hat, and Sourcegraph, and the protocol is becoming supported by a rapidly growing list of editor and language communities.

Here are the search results for Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) and cross-editor debugging:

## Overview of DAP

The Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) defines the abstract protocol used between a development tool (e.g., IDE or editor) and a debugger.(no content)Adding a debugger for a new language to an IDE or editor is not only a significant effort, but it is also frustrating that this effort cannot be easily amortized over multiple development tools, as each tool uses different APIs for implementing the same feature. The idea behind DAP is to abstract the way how the debugging support of development tools communicates with debuggers or runtimes into a protocol.(no content)Since it is unrealistic to assume that existing debuggers or runtimes adopt this protocol any time soon, an intermediary component - a so-called Debug Adapter - adapts an existing debugger or runtime to the Debug Adapter Protocol. This makes it possible to implement a generic debugger for a development tool that can communicate with different debuggers via Debug Adapters.

## Cross-Editor Support

Many adapters publish releases tailored for specific editors, such as VS Code, and some document how to run standalone DAP servers which can be used in any editor which supports them (in IntelliJ or VS Code for example).(no content)The diagram shows that the Debug Adapter Protocol makes it possible to implement a single generic debugger UI per development tool and that Debug Adapters can be re-used across these tools.

## JetBrains Support via LSP4IJ

LSP4IJ is a free and open-source Language Server Protocol (LSP) client for JetBrains-based IDEs, compatible with both community and enterprise flavors. The latest version of LSP4IJ includes a new, free and open-source Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) client for JetBrains-based IDEs, compatible with both community and enterprise flavors.(no content)To debug JavaScript or TypeScript files, you can use the VSCode JS Debug DAP server. Download the js-debug-dap-v*.tar.gz asset from the VSCode JS Debug releases. For example, download js-debug-dap-v1.96.0.tar.gz, which is the latest version at the time of writing.

## VS Code Implementation

VS Code calls this intermediary the Debug Adapter (or DA for short) and the abstract protocol used between the DA and VS Code is the Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP for short). Since the Debug Adapter Protocol is independent from VS Code, it has its own web site where you can find an introduction and overview, the detailed specification, and some lists with known implementations and supporting tools.(no content)Since debug adapters are independent from VS Code and can be used in other development tools, they do not match VS Code's extensibility architecture which is based on extensions and contribution points.

## Benefits of DAP

Applications developed using LSP (provides language intelligence) or DAP (provides debugging) ensures a common experience. If you provide software extensions (plugins, adapters) using both LSP and DAP then your dev-tools (Eclipse Theia, VS Code, or other IDE) become familiar tools for any developer who wants to program in any language.

Here are the search results for Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) and cross-editor debugging:

## Overview of DAP

The Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) defines the abstract protocol used between a development tool (e.g., IDE or editor) and a debugger.(no content)Adding a debugger for a new language to an IDE or editor is not only a significant effort, but it is also frustrating that this effort cannot be easily amortized over multiple development tools, as each tool uses different APIs for implementing the same feature. The idea behind DAP is to abstract the way how the debugging support of development tools communicates with debuggers or runtimes into a protocol.(no content)Since it is unrealistic to assume that existing debuggers or runtimes adopt this protocol any time soon, an intermediary component - a so-called Debug Adapter - adapts an existing debugger or runtime to the Debug Adapter Protocol. This makes it possible to implement a generic debugger for a development tool that can communicate with different debuggers via Debug Adapters.

## Cross-Editor Support

Many adapters publish releases tailored for specific editors, such as VS Code, and some document how to run standalone DAP servers which can be used in any editor which supports them (in IntelliJ or VS Code for example).(no content)The diagram shows that the Debug Adapter Protocol makes it possible to implement a single generic debugger UI per development tool and that Debug Adapters can be re-used across these tools.

## JetBrains Support via LSP4IJ

LSP4IJ is a free and open-source Language Server Protocol (LSP) client for JetBrains-based IDEs, compatible with both community and enterprise flavors. The latest version of LSP4IJ includes a new, free and open-source Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP) client for JetBrains-based IDEs, compatible with both community and enterprise flavors.(no content)To debug JavaScript or TypeScript files, you can use the VSCode JS Debug DAP server. Download the js-debug-dap-v*.tar.gz asset from the VSCode JS Debug releases. For example, download js-debug-dap-v1.96.0.tar.gz, which is the latest version at the time of writing.

## VS Code Implementation

VS Code calls this intermediary the Debug Adapter (or DA for short) and the abstract protocol used between the DA and VS Code is the Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP for short). Since the Debug Adapter Protocol is independent from VS Code, it has its own web site where you can find an introduction and overview, the detailed specification, and some lists with known implementations and supporting tools.(no content)Since debug adapters are independent from VS Code and can be used in other development tools, they do not match VS Code's extensibility architecture which is based on extensions and contribution points.

## Benefits of DAP

Applications developed using LSP (provides language intelligence) or DAP (provides debugging) ensures a common experience. If you provide software extensions (plugins, adapters) using both LSP and DAP then your dev-tools (Eclipse Theia, VS Code, or other IDE) become familiar tools for any developer who wants to program in any language.

Here's a comprehensive overview of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and its integration with AI tools and editors in 2025:

## What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard and open-source framework introduced by Anthropic in November 2024 to standardize the way artificial intelligence (AI) systems like large language models (LLMs) integrate and share data with external tools, systems, and data sources.(no content)MCP provides a universal interface for reading files, executing functions, and handling contextual prompts.

## Key Adoption in 2025

In March 2025, OpenAI officially adopted the MCP, after having integrated the standard across its products, including the ChatGPT desktop app. MCP can be integrated with Microsoft Semantic Kernel, and Azure OpenAI.(no content)In December 2025, Anthropic donated the MCP to the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), a directed fund under the Linux Foundation, co-founded by Anthropic, Block and OpenAI, with support from other companies.

## Editor & Development Tool Integration

The protocol is used in AI-assisted software development tools. Integrated development environments (IDEs), coding platforms such as Replit, and code intelligence tools like Sourcegraph have adopted MCP to grant AI coding assistants real-time access to project context.(no content)Zed has integrated MCP capabilities to enhance their code editor with sophisticated AI-powered development assistance. This integration enables AI systems to access project context, version control information, documentation, and external development tools to provide more accurate and relevant coding assistance.(no content)Examples of MCP clients are IBM® BeeAI, Microsoft Copilot Studio, Claude.ai, Windsurf Editor and Postman.

## How It Works

You can think of MCP for AI applications to serve the same purpose as a USB-C port serves for hardware—highlighting the adaptability for connecting various tools and data sources to AI models through a standardized interface.(no content)MCP re-uses the message-flow ideas of the Language Server Protocol (LSP) and is transported over JSON-RPC 2.0.

## Security Concerns

The rapid adoption of MCP has opened numerous, critical security challenges. Research by Knostic in July 2025 involved scanning nearly 2,000 MCP servers exposed to the internet, with all verified servers lacking any form of authentication.(no content)The June 2025 update to the MCP authorization specification addresses some concerns by classifying MCP servers as OAuth Resource Servers, while requiring clients to implement Resource Indicators (RFC 8707).

## Impact on the Industry

Although the Model Context Protocol (MCP) was launched in November 2024, it's played a significant role in driving AI adoption and experimentation forward. It's arguably brought agentic AI into the mainstream much faster than the industry may have expected — by making it easier for developers to connect agents to many different sources of data.

Here are the search results for Zed editor extension development with WebAssembly/WASM plugins:

## Overview

Procedural parts of extensions are written in Rust and compiled to WebAssembly.(no content)Zed extensions are Rust programs compiled to WebAssembly that can provide slash commands, language support, themes, grammars, and MCP servers. Extensions implement the `zed::Extension` trait and are distributed via Zed's extension registry.

## Architecture & Technical Details

Extensions are built on the WebAssembly Component Model, which allows defining interfaces (APIs) between Wasm modules.(no content)The Zed team found the WebAssembly Component Model had only recently become usable and "got wasmtime and the Wasm component model plugged in. And everything just sort of clicked together at that point."(no content)Once downloaded, the Zed editor throws them inside of two wasm files: one for the tree-sitter grammar and extension.wasm for the actual extension. Notably, the LSP is downloaded and executed as an executable outside of the wasm.(no content)The Wasm extensions are loaded and run using the Wasm runtime called Wasmtime.

## Getting Started with Development

Before starting to develop an extension for Zed, be sure to install Rust via rustup. Rust must be installed via rustup. If you have Rust installed via homebrew or otherwise, installing dev extensions will not work.(no content)When developing an extension, you can use it in Zed without needing to publish it by installing it as a dev extension. From the extensions page, click the Install Dev Extension button and select the directory containing your extension.

## Extension Capabilities

An extension in Zed is a way to extend the editor's functionality with things such as language support, themes, MCP servers, debugger protocols, and others. For now, extensions can add language support, debuggers, MCP servers, themes, and icon themes. In the future, they plan on increasing the extension surface to allow customizing Zed's UI and more.(no content)Currently the extension API only allows users to add new language support, themes, snippets, and slash commands. There's no support for modifying the UI to create new panels, or making arbitrary HTTP requests, or touching the file system how you want.

## Why WebAssembly?

For Zed, they wanted a smooth, safe plugin-installation experience that didn't depend on the user's C compiler. They wanted to make it impossible for an extension to crash Zed. Tree-sitter parsers contain code that can have bugs, and they would never be able to prevent crashes if they loaded extensions directly as shared libraries.(no content)WebAssembly (or wasm for short) is a great format for distributing parsers, because it's cross-platform, and it's designed for running untrusted code safely.(no content)A plugin written in wasm would be fast, allow for plugins to be written in Rust and also be multiplatform so only one target is required for a plugin to be used everywhere.

## Key Resources

- **Official Documentation**: https://zed.dev/docs/extensions/developing-extensions
- **Extensions Repository**: https://github.com/zed-industries/extensions
- **Blog Post on Extensions**: https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions

## VSCode Extension API Overview

"Here are some examples of what you can achieve with the Extension API: Change the look of VS Code with a color or file icon theme - Theming · Add custom components & views in the UI - Extending the Workbench · Create a Webview to display a custom webpage built with HTML/CSS/JS - Webview Guide · Support a new programming language - Language Extensions Overview · Support debugging a specific runtime - Debugger Extension Guide."

### Webview API

The `vscode.window.createWebviewPanel` function creates and shows a webview in the editor.(no content)Webviews are a powerful way to add custom functionality beyond what the Visual Studio Code API supports. They're fully customizable, which, historically, has meant that the responsibility of developing UI which aligns with the Visual Studio Code design language lies in the hands of extension authors.

Key webview characteristics:
- VS Code's webviews render arbitrary HTML in a sandbox; a bit like a specialized `<iframe>` element.
- Webviews are sandboxed from normal extension process, so all communication with the webview must use message passing.
- VS Code exposes an API to webview content that lets them communicate with VS Code using postMessage. They register custom handlers inside of webviews to intercept clicks and other actions.

### Language Features

Programmatic Language Features add rich programming language support such as Hovers, Go to Definition, diagnostic errors, IntelliSense and CodeLens. These language features are exposed through the `vscode.languages.*` API. An extension can either use these APIs directly, or write a Language Server and adapt it to VS Code using the VS Code Language Server library.(no content)Extensions can add folding, breadcrumbs and outline support for a language.

### Important 2025 Update

The Webview UI Toolkit for VS Code will be deprecated on January 1, 2025. See the deprecation announcement for more details.

### Performance Considerations

We strongly encourage you to carefully review whether or not your extension needs to use webviews before building. While webviews provide a great way to add custom functionality, oftentimes, they come at the cost of performance and accessibility. The core Visual Studio Code API provides a vast array of building blocks for highly performant, accessible, and tightly integrated extension experiences.

- Official Webview API docs: https://code.visualstudio.com/api/extension-guides/webview
- Extension Capabilities Overview: https://code.visualstudio.com/api/extension-capabilities/overview
- Sample extensions: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-extension-samples

Here are the search results for unified plugin APIs and cross-editor/IDE abstraction frameworks:

## Key Findings

### 1. JetBrains UAST (Unified Abstract Syntax Tree)
UAST (Unified Abstract Syntax Tree) is an abstraction layer on the PSI of different programming languages targeting the JVM (Java Virtual Machine). It provides a unified API for working with common language elements like classes and methods.(no content)UAST provides a unified way to represent JVM compatible declarations via UMethod, UField, UClass, and so on.

### 2. Language Server Protocol (LSP) as Cross-Editor Solution
Each editor has its own plugin API, which need to be separately developed and maintained. In addition WebStorm and Vim run on non-javascript languages so there would need to be some extra care taken to handle binding to the JavaScript API/CLI.(no content)One promising potential solution is the language server protocol, which extracts language analysis out of the editor process into a separate process, and communicates through a standard JSON RPC interface.(no content)The protocol appears to support many of the features implemented by unified editor integrations already: reporting issues inline in the IDE and autofixing code.

### 3. unified.js (Content Processing Framework)
You can connect utilities together and make your own plugins that check for problems and transform from one thing to another. When you are dealing with one type of content (such as markdown), you can use the main package of that ecosystem instead.(no content)The syntax trees used in unified are unist nodes. A tree represents a whole document and each node is a plain JavaScript object with a type field.

### 4. SAL.Windows Plugin Architecture
SAL.Windows offers a plugin-based architecture with support for multiple UI types (MDI/SDI/Dialog/VS IDE integration), centralized settings management, dynamic module loading, and interface abstraction for Windows Forms controls and menus, with cross-platform compatibility for .NET Framework 3.5 and .NET Standard 2.0.

### 5. IntelliJ Platform
The IntelliJ Platform provides all the infrastructure that these IDEs need to provide rich language tooling support. It is a component-driven, cross-platform JVM-based application host with a high-level user interface toolkit for creating tool windows, tree views, and lists.

### 6. Graphics API Abstraction (Related Example)
Diligent Engine is a light-weight cross-platform graphics API abstraction layer designed to take advantages of the next-generation APIs such as Direct3D12 and Vulkan, but at the same time provide support for older platforms via Direct3D11, OpenGL and OpenGLES.

---

**Summary**: The **Language Server Protocol (LSP)** appears to be the most widely adopted solution for creating truly cross-editor plugin functionality, as it abstracts language features into a separate process that communicates via standard JSON RPC. For JVM-based IDEs specifically, **UAST** provides unified syntax tree abstraction within the IntelliJ ecosystem.

## Key Findings

### 1. JetBrains UAST (Unified Abstract Syntax Tree)
UAST (Unified Abstract Syntax Tree) is an abstraction layer on the PSI of different programming languages targeting the JVM (Java Virtual Machine). It provides a unified API for working with common language elements like classes and methods.(no content)UAST provides a unified way to represent JVM compatible declarations via UMethod, UField, UClass, and so on.

### 2. Language Server Protocol (LSP) as Cross-Editor Solution
Each editor has its own plugin API, which need to be separately developed and maintained. In addition WebStorm and Vim run on non-javascript languages so there would need to be some extra care taken to handle binding to the JavaScript API/CLI.(no content)One promising potential solution is the language server protocol, which extracts language analysis out of the editor process into a separate process, and communicates through a standard JSON RPC interface.(no content)The protocol appears to support many of the features implemented by unified editor integrations already: reporting issues inline in the IDE and autofixing code.

### 3. unified.js (Content Processing Framework)
You can connect utilities together and make your own plugins that check for problems and transform from one thing to another. When you are dealing with one type of content (such as markdown), you can use the main package of that ecosystem instead.(no content)The syntax trees used in unified are unist nodes. A tree represents a whole document and each node is a plain JavaScript object with a type field.

### 4. SAL.Windows Plugin Architecture
SAL.Windows offers a plugin-based architecture with support for multiple UI types (MDI/SDI/Dialog/VS IDE integration), centralized settings management, dynamic module loading, and interface abstraction for Windows Forms controls and menus, with cross-platform compatibility for .NET Framework 3.5 and .NET Standard 2.0.

### 5. IntelliJ Platform
The IntelliJ Platform provides all the infrastructure that these IDEs need to provide rich language tooling support. It is a component-driven, cross-platform JVM-based application host with a high-level user interface toolkit for creating tool windows, tree views, and lists.

### 6. Graphics API Abstraction (Related Example)
Diligent Engine is a light-weight cross-platform graphics API abstraction layer designed to take advantages of the next-generation APIs such as Direct3D12 and Vulkan, but at the same time provide support for older platforms via Direct3D11, OpenGL and OpenGLES.

---

**Summary**: The **Language Server Protocol (LSP)** appears to be the most widely adopted solution for creating truly cross-editor plugin functionality, as it abstracts language features into a separate process that communicates via standard JSON RPC. For JVM-based IDEs specifically, **UAST** provides unified syntax tree abstraction within the IntelliJ ecosystem.

1. How to create plugins for JetBrains IDEs
2. Whether there's an API that allows creating plugins for multiple editors (VSCode, Zed, JetBrains) at the same time - like React Native/Tauri do for cross-platform

Based on my research, here are the key findings:

## Cross-Platform Plugin Development Options

### 1. **Language Server Protocol (LSP)** - The de facto standard
- Created by Microsoft
- Allows language features (autocomplete, go-to-definition, diagnostics, etc.) to work across any LSP-compatible editor
- Supported by: VSCode, JetBrains IDEs (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Sublime, Neovim, Emacs, Eclipse, etc.
- Write once, use everywhere for language intelligence features

### 2. **Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP)** - For debugging
- Similar concept to LSP but for debugging
- JetBrains recently got support via LSP4IJ
- Allows cross-editor debugging capabilities

### 3. **Model Context Protocol (MCP)** - For AI integration
- Created by Anthropic
- Adopted by OpenAI, Zed, and many others
- Provides universal interface for AI tools to integrate with editors

### 4. **Agent Client Protocol (ACP)** - Emerging standard
- Collaboration between JetBrains and Zed
- For AI coding agents to work across editors
- Full support coming to JetBrains IDEs, already in Zed
- VSCode support uncertain (Microsoft is considering)

## Editor-Specific Approaches

### JetBrains
- Uses IntelliJ Platform SDK
- Kotlin/Java-based plugins
- Gradle-based build system
- Can use LSP via LSP4IJ

### VSCode


- JavaScript/TypeScript extensions
- Rich API with webviews, language features, etc.
- Native LSP support

### Zed
- WebAssembly (Rust compiled to WASM)
- Sandboxed execution
- LSP for language features
- MCP for AI integration
- ACP for agent integration

## The Answer to "Is there something like React Native/Tauri for IDE plugins?"

**Not exactly**, but the closest options are:

1. **LSP** - For language features (autocomplete, diagnostics, etc.)
2. **DAP** - For debugging
3. **MCP** - For AI tools
4. **ACP** - For AI coding agents

These protocols let me write core functionality once and deploy across multiple editors, though each still requires some editor-specific wrapper code. LSP is the most mature and widely supported, making it the best choice for maximum compatibility.

The fundamental challenge is that editors have vastly different architectures and UI systems—VS Code's webview-based approach differs significantly from Neovim's Lua scripting or Zed's WASM model. Deep editor integration (custom commands, keybindings, UI panels) remains editor-specific, so a true write-once-run-anywhere solution like React Native doesn't exist for IDE plugins.

# Research: Cross-Platform IDE Plugin Development

## Executive Summary

**Não existe um framework unificado tipo "React Native para plugins de IDE"**, mas existem **protocolos padronizados** que permitem compartilhar a lógica core entre editores diferentes. A estratégia recomendada é usar esses protocolos com thin adapters por editor.

---

## Protocolos Cross-Platform Disponíveis

### 1. Language Server Protocol (LSP) ⭐ Mais Maduro
**O padrão de facto para features de linguagem cross-editor.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Microsoft |
| Versão atual | 3.17 |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC 2.0 |
| Suporte | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim, Emacs, Sublime, Eclipse |

**Features cobertas:**
- Autocomplete / IntelliSense
- Go to Definition / References
- Hover documentation
- Diagnostics (errors, warnings)
- Code Actions / Quick Fixes
- Rename / Refactor
- Formatting

**Arquitetura:**
```
┌─────────────────┐     JSON-RPC      ┌──────────────────┐
│   VSCode        │◄──────────────────►│                  │
├─────────────────┤                    │  Language Server │
│   JetBrains     │◄──────────────────►│  (seu código)    │
├─────────────────┤                    │                  │
│   Zed           │◄──────────────────►│                  │
└─────────────────┘                    └──────────────────┘
```

**Benefício:** Escreva um Language Server uma vez, funciona em todos os editores LSP-compatíveis.

**Links:**
- [LSP Official](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/)
- [JetBrains LSP Support](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/language-server-protocol.html)
- [LSP4IJ (JetBrains)](https://idetools.dev/blog/lsp4ij-dap-announcement/)

---

### 2. Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP)
**O LSP equivalente para debugging.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Microsoft |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC |
| Suporte | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim |

**Features cobertas:**
- Breakpoints
- Step execution
- Variable inspection
- Call stack
- Watch expressions

**Links:**
- [DAP Official](https://microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol/)

---

### 3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) ⭐ Para AI
**Padrão para integração de AI com ferramentas.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Anthropic (Nov 2024) |
| Adotado por | OpenAI (Mar 2025), Zed, IBM BeeAI, Windsurf |
| Status | Doado à Linux Foundation (Dec 2025) |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC 2.0 (baseado em LSP) |

**Features cobertas:**
- Acesso a contexto de projeto
- Execução de ferramentas
- Prompts customizados
- Resources (arquivos, dados)

**Links:**
- [MCP Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25)
- [Anthropic Announcement](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol)

---

### 4. Agent Client Protocol (ACP) 🆕 Emergente
**Protocolo para AI coding agents rodarem em múltiplos IDEs.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Zed + JetBrains (colaboração) |
| Status | Em desenvolvimento ativo |
| Suporte atual | Zed (nativo), JetBrains (coming soon), Emacs (plugin), Neovim (plugins) |
| VSCode | Incerto (em discussão) |

**Benefícios:**
- Agents editor-agnostic
- Sem vendor lock-in
- UX nativa em cada IDE

**Links:**
- [Zed ACP Docs](https://zed.dev/acp)
- [JetBrains × Zed Announcement](https://blog.jetbrains.com/ai/2025/10/jetbrains-zed-open-interoperability-for-ai-coding-agents-in-your-ide/)

---

## Arquiteturas Por Editor

### JetBrains IDEs

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: Kotlin/Java
- Build: Gradle + IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin 2.x
- Runtime: JVM

**Mudanças 2025.3:**
- IntelliJ IDEA Community e Ultimate unificados
- Use `intellijIdea()` dependency helper
- Plugin DevKit melhorado com Kotlin Notebook integration

**Para usar LSP:**
```kotlin
// Via LSP4IJ - open source client
// Permite usar Language Servers existentes
```

**Links:**
- [IntelliJ Platform SDK](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/developing-plugins.html)
- [Quick Start Guide](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/plugins-quick-start.html)

---

### VSCode

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: TypeScript/JavaScript
- Build: npm/webpack/esbuild
- Runtime: Node.js (extension host)

**Capabilities:**
- Language features (LSP nativo)
- Webviews (UI customizada)
- Tree views, panels
- Commands, keybindings
- Debug adapters

**Links:**
- [Extension API](https://code.visualstudio.com/api)
- [Language Server Guide](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/language-extensions/language-server-extension-guide)

---

### Zed

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: Rust → WebAssembly
- Runtime: Wasmtime (sandboxed)
- Build: cargo + wasm target

**Capabilities atuais:**
- Language support (via LSP)
- Themes
- Slash commands
- MCP servers
- Debugger protocols (DAP)

**Limitações:**
- Sem modificação de UI (panels customizados)
- Sem HTTP requests arbitrários
- Filesystem limitado

**Links:**
- [Developing Extensions](https://zed.dev/docs/extensions/developing-extensions)
- [Extensions Blog Post](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions)

---

## Estratégia Recomendada: Arquitetura Multi-Camada

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Core Logic Layer                     │
│         (TypeScript/Rust - business logic)              │
│                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  │
│  │ LSP Server  │  │ DAP Server   │  │ MCP Server    │  │
│  └─────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
          ▼                ▼                ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│   VSCode     │  │  JetBrains   │  │     Zed      │
│   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │
│  (thin TS)   │  │ (thin Kotlin)│  │ (thin Rust)  │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
```

### Componentes:

1. **Core Logic (compartilhado):**
   - Language Server (LSP)
   - Debug Adapter (DAP)
   - MCP Server (se AI features)
   - Pode ser em TypeScript, Rust, ou qualquer linguagem

2. **Thin Adapters (por editor):**
   - VSCode: Package Extension que spawna o server
   - JetBrains: Plugin que usa LSP4IJ ou custom integration
   - Zed: WASM extension que configura o LSP

---

## Comparação: Cross-Platform Frameworks vs IDE Plugins

| Aspecto | React Native/Tauri | IDE Plugins |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Modelo | Framework unificado | Protocolos + adapters |
| Código compartilhado | 90%+ | 60-80% (via LSP/DAP) |
| UI | Abstração componentes | Específica por editor |
| Build | Único toolchain | Múltiplos toolchains |
| Deployment | Um artefato | Artefato por editor |

**Por que não existe um "React Native para IDEs":**
- IDEs têm APIs de UI muito diferentes
- Deep integration requer acesso a features específicas
- Ecossistemas de plugins estabelecidos (marketplaces)
- Diferentes modelos de segurança (sandboxing)

---

## Conclusão

**Para features de linguagem:** Use **LSP** - máximo reuso de código

**Para debugging:** Use **DAP** - mesma ideia que LSP

**Para AI integration:** Use **MCP** e/ou **ACP**

**Para UI customizada:** Inevitavelmente precisará de código específico por editor

A melhor estratégia é investir no core logic (Language Server, ferramentas) e criar thin adapters para cada plataforma alvo.

---

## Próximos Passos Sugeridos

1. [ ] Definir quais features o plugin precisa ter
2. [ ] Identificar quais protocolos cobrem essas features
3. [ ] Escolher linguagem para o core (TypeScript recomendado para LSP)
4. [ ] Criar proof-of-concept com LSP para um editor
5. [ ] Expandir para outros editores via adapters

## Resumo da Pesquisa

Pesquisei extensivamente sobre desenvolvimento de plugins cross-platform para IDEs. Aqui está o que encontrei:

### Não Existe um "React Native para Plugins de IDE" 😕

Infelizmente, não há um framework unificado que permita escrever um único código e deployar para VSCode, JetBrains e Zed simultaneamente.

**Porém**, existem **protocolos padronizados** que permitem compartilhar 60-80% do código:

### Protocolos Cross-Platform

| Protocolo | Propósito | Suporte |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **LSP** (Language Server Protocol) | Autocomplete, Go to Definition, Diagnostics | VSCode, JetBrains, Zed, Neovim, Emacs, Sublime |
| **DAP** (Debug Adapter Protocol) | Debugging | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed |
| **MCP** (Model Context Protocol) | Integração AI | Claude, ChatGPT, Zed, Windsurf |
| **ACP** (Agent Client Protocol) 🆕 | AI coding agents | Zed, JetBrains (coming soon), VSCode (incerto) |

### Estratégia Recomendada

```
Core Logic (LSP/DAP/MCP Server) → TypeScript ou Rust
        ↓
Thin Adapters por Editor (código mínimo específico)
```

content.text:
Correto! Vou esclarecer:

## LSP e UI

**LSP NÃO lida com UI.** Ele apenas define o protocolo de comunicação para features de linguagem (dados). A renderização é 100% responsabilidade do editor.

```
LSP Server → "Há um erro na linha 42"
Editor → Decide COMO mostrar (sublinhado vermelho, ícone, tooltip, etc.)
```

## MCP e ACP - Também NÃO lidam com UI diretamente

| Protocolo | O que define | UI |
|-----------|--------------|-----|
| **MCP** | Como AI acessa ferramentas/contexto | ❌ Não |
| **ACP** | Como agents interagem com o editor | ⚠️ Parcialmente |

### ACP tem algo interessante:

O ACP define **ações semânticas** que o agent pode pedir ao editor, como:
- Mostrar diff para aprovação
- Solicitar confirmação do usuário
- Streaming de updates

Mas a **renderização visual** dessas ações ainda é específica de cada editor. O ACP padroniza o "o quê", não o "como mostrar".

Boa pergunta! Achei mais detalhes:

## ACP (Agent Client Protocol) - Tem Suporte a UI! ✅

O ACP é mais avançado que LSP/MCP nesse aspecto. Ele define **elementos de UI específicos para coding agents**:

### O que o ACP padroniza em termos de UI:

| Feature | Descrição |
|---------|-----------|
| **Multi-file diffs** | Visualização de mudanças em múltiplos arquivos |
| **Live progress** | Streaming em tempo real do que o agent está fazendo |
| **Agent following** | Editor "segue" o agent pelo codebase (mostra onde está lendo/editando) |
| **Rich Markdown** | Todo texto human-readable é Markdown por padrão |
| **Patches UI** | Componentes específicos para aprovar/rejeitar mudanças |

### Mas ainda é "semântico", não "visual"

```
ACP define: "Mostre um diff de 3 arquivos para aprovação"
Editor decide: Como renderizar (side-by-side, inline, cores, etc.)
```

O ACP é descrito como **"LSP for AI Coding Agents"** - define as interações, mas cada editor implementa a renderização.

### Editores com suporte ACP hoje:
- **Zed** (nativo, melhor suporte)
- **Neovim** (via plugins)
- **Marimo**
- **JetBrains** (em desenvolvimento)

### Agents compatíveis:
- Claude Code
- Codex CLI
- Gemini
- Goose
- StackPack

**Sources:**
- [ACP GitHub Spec](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/agent-client-protocol)
- [Zed ACP Docs](https://zed.dev/acp)
- [JetBrains ACP Docs](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/ai-assistant/acp.html)
- [ACP Introduction (Block/Goose)](https://block.github.io/goose/blog/2025/10/24/intro-to-agent-client-protocol-acp/)

---

**Resumindo:** Se você quer UI cross-editor para AI agents, **ACP é o mais próximo que existe** de um padrão. Mas para UI genérica (painéis customizados, botões, etc.), ainda não há solução unificada.

input.plan:
# Research: Cross-Platform IDE Plugin Development

## Executive Summary

**Não existe um framework unificado tipo "React Native para plugins de IDE"**, mas existem **protocolos padronizados** que permitem compartilhar a lógica core entre editores diferentes. A estratégia recomendada é usar esses protocolos com thin adapters por editor.

---

## Protocolos Cross-Platform Disponíveis

### 1. Language Server Protocol (LSP) ⭐ Mais Maduro
**O padrão de facto para features de linguagem cross-editor.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Microsoft |
| Versão atual | 3.17 |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC 2.0 |
| Suporte | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim, Emacs, Sublime, Eclipse |

**Features cobertas:**
- Autocomplete / IntelliSense
- Go to Definition / References
- Hover documentation
- Diagnostics (errors, warnings)
- Code Actions / Quick Fixes
- Rename / Refactor
- Formatting

**Arquitetura:**
```
┌─────────────────┐     JSON-RPC      ┌──────────────────┐
│   VSCode        │◄──────────────────►│                  │
├─────────────────┤                    │  Language Server │
│   JetBrains     │◄──────────────────►│  (seu código)    │
├─────────────────┤                    │                  │
│   Zed           │◄──────────────────►│                  │
└─────────────────┘                    └──────────────────┘
```

**Benefício:** Escreva um Language Server uma vez, funciona em todos os editores LSP-compatíveis.

**Links:**
- [LSP Official](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/)
- [JetBrains LSP Support](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/language-server-protocol.html)
- [LSP4IJ (JetBrains)](https://idetools.dev/blog/lsp4ij-dap-announcement/)

---

### 2. Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP)
**O LSP equivalente para debugging.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Microsoft |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC |
| Suporte | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim |

**Features cobertas:**
- Breakpoints
- Step execution
- Variable inspection
- Call stack
- Watch expressions

**Links:**
- [DAP Official](https://microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol/)

---

### 3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) ⭐ Para AI
**Padrão para integração de AI com ferramentas.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Anthropic (Nov 2024) |
| Adotado por | OpenAI (Mar 2025), Zed, IBM BeeAI, Windsurf |
| Status | Doado à Linux Foundation (Dec 2025) |
| Transporte | JSON-RPC 2.0 (baseado em LSP) |

**Features cobertas:**
- Acesso a contexto de projeto
- Execução de ferramentas
- Prompts customizados
- Resources (arquivos, dados)

**Links:**
- [MCP Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25)
- [Anthropic Announcement](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol)

---

### 4. Agent Client Protocol (ACP) 🆕 Emergente
**Protocolo para AI coding agents rodarem em múltiplos IDEs.**

| Aspecto | Detalhes |
|---------|----------|
| Criado por | Zed + JetBrains (colaboração) |
| Status | Em desenvolvimento ativo |
| Suporte atual | Zed (nativo), JetBrains (coming soon), Emacs (plugin), Neovim (plugins) |
| VSCode | Incerto (em discussão) |

**Benefícios:**
- Agents editor-agnostic
- Sem vendor lock-in
- UX nativa em cada IDE

**Links:**
- [Zed ACP Docs](https://zed.dev/acp)
- [JetBrains × Zed Announcement](https://blog.jetbrains.com/ai/2025/10/jetbrains-zed-open-interoperability-for-ai-coding-agents-in-your-ide/)

---

## Arquiteturas Por Editor

### JetBrains IDEs

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: Kotlin/Java
- Build: Gradle + IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin 2.x
- Runtime: JVM

**Mudanças 2025.3:**
- IntelliJ IDEA Community e Ultimate unificados
- Use `intellijIdea()` dependency helper
- Plugin DevKit melhorado com Kotlin Notebook integration

**Para usar LSP:**
```kotlin
// Via LSP4IJ - open source client
// Permite usar Language Servers existentes
```

**Links:**
- [IntelliJ Platform SDK](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/developing-plugins.html)
- [Quick Start Guide](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/plugins-quick-start.html)

---

### VSCode

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: TypeScript/JavaScript
- Build: npm/webpack/esbuild
- Runtime: Node.js (extension host)

**Capabilities:**
- Language features (LSP nativo)
- Webviews (UI customizada)
- Tree views, panels
- Commands, keybindings
- Debug adapters

**Links:**
- [Extension API](https://code.visualstudio.com/api)
- [Language Server Guide](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/language-extensions/language-server-extension-guide)

---

### Zed

**Stack:**
- Linguagem: Rust → WebAssembly
- Runtime: Wasmtime (sandboxed)
- Build: cargo + wasm target

**Capabilities atuais:**
- Language support (via LSP)
- Themes
- Slash commands
- MCP servers
- Debugger protocols (DAP)

**Limitações:**
- Sem modificação de UI (panels customizados)
- Sem HTTP requests arbitrários
- Filesystem limitado

**Links:**
- [Developing Extensions](https://zed.dev/docs/extensions/developing-extensions)
- [Extensions Blog Post](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions)

---

## Estratégia Recomendada: Arquitetura Multi-Camada

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Core Logic Layer                     │
│         (TypeScript/Rust - business logic)              │
│                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  │
│  │ LSP Server  │  │ DAP Server   │  │ MCP Server    │  │
│  └─────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
          ▼                ▼                ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│   VSCode     │  │  JetBrains   │  │     Zed      │
│   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │
│  (thin TS)   │  │ (thin Kotlin)│  │ (thin Rust)  │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
```

### Componentes:

1. **Core Logic (compartilhado):**
   - Language Server (LSP)
   - Debug Adapter (DAP)
   - MCP Server (se AI features)
   - Pode ser em TypeScript, Rust, ou qualquer linguagem

2. **Thin Adapters (por editor):**
   - VSCode: Package Extension que spawna o server
   - JetBrains: Plugin que usa LSP4IJ ou custom integration
   - Zed: WASM extension que configura o LSP

---

## Comparação: Cross-Platform Frameworks vs IDE Plugins

| Aspecto | React Native/Tauri | IDE Plugins |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Modelo | Framework unificado | Protocolos + adapters |
| Código compartilhado | 90%+ | 60-80% (via LSP/DAP) |
| UI | Abstração componentes | Específica por editor |
| Build | Único toolchain | Múltiplos toolchains |
| Deployment | Um artefato | Artefato por editor |

**Por que não existe um "React Native para IDEs":**
- IDEs têm APIs de UI muito diferentes
- Deep integration requer acesso a features específicas
- Ecossistemas de plugins estabelecidos (marketplaces)
- Diferentes modelos de segurança (sandboxing)

---

## Conclusão

**Para features de linguagem:** Use **LSP** - máximo reuso de código

**Para debugging:** Use **DAP** - mesma ideia que LSP

**Para AI integration:** Use **MCP** e/ou **ACP**

**Para UI customizada:** Inevitavelmente precisará de código específico por editor

A melhor estratégia é investir no core logic (Language Server, ferramentas) e criar thin adapters para cada plataforma alvo.

---

## Próximos Passos Sugeridos

1. [ ] Definir quais features o plugin precisa ter
2. [ ] Identificar quais protocolos cobrem essas features
3. [ ] Escolher linguagem para o core (TypeScript recomendado para LSP)
4. [ ] Criar proof-of-concept com LSP para um editor
5. [ ] Expandir para outros editores via adapters

- Runtime: Wasmtime (sandboxed)
- Build: cargo + wasm target

**Capabilities atuais:**
- Language support (via LSP)
- Themes
- Slash commands
- MCP servers
- Debugger protocols (DAP)

**Limitações:**
- Sem modificação de UI (panels customizados)
- Sem HTTP requests arbitrários
- Filesystem limitado

**Links:**
- [Developing Extensions](https://zed.dev/docs/extensions/developing-extensions)
- [Extensions Blog Post](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions)

---

## Estratégia Recomendada: Arquitetura Multi-Camada

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Core Logic Layer                     │
│         (TypeScript/Rust - business logic)              │
│                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  │
│  │ LSP Server  │  │ DAP Server   │  │ MCP Server    │  │
│  └─────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                           │
          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
          ▼                ▼                ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│   VSCode     │  │  JetBrains   │  │     Zed      │
│   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │  │   Adapter    │
│  (thin TS)   │  │ (thin Kotlin)│  │ (thin Rust)  │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
```

### Componentes:

1. **Core Logic (compartilhado):**
   - Language Server (LSP)
   - Debug Adapter (DAP)
   - MCP Server (se AI features)
   - Pode ser em TypeScript, Rust, ou qualquer linguagem

2. **Thin Adapters (por editor):**
   - VSCode: Package Extension que spawna o server
   - JetBrains: Plugin que usa LSP4IJ ou custom integration
   - Zed: WASM extension que configura o LSP

---

## Comparação: Cross-Platform Frameworks vs IDE Plugins

| Aspecto | React Native/Tauri | IDE Plugins |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Modelo | Framework unificado | Protocolos + adapters |
| Código compartilhado | 90%+ | 60-80% (via LSP/DAP) |
| UI | Abstração componentes | Específica por editor |
| Build | Único toolchain | Múltiplos toolchains |
| Deployment | Um artefato | Artefato por editor |

**Por que não existe um "React Native para IDEs":**
- IDEs têm APIs de UI muito diferentes
- Deep integration requer acesso a features específicas
- Ecossistemas de plugins estabelecidos (marketplaces)
- Diferentes modelos de segurança (sandboxing)

---

## Conclusão

**Para features de linguagem:** Use **LSP** - máximo reuso de código

**Para debugging:** Use **DAP** - mesma ideia que LSP

**Para AI integration:** Use **MCP** e/ou **ACP**

**Para UI customizada:** Inevitavelmente precisará de código específico por editor

A melhor estratégia é investir no core logic (Language Server, ferramentas) e criar thin adapters para cada plataforma alvo.

---

3. [ ] Escolher linguagem para o core (TypeScript recomendado para LSP)
4. [ ] Criar proof-of-concept com LSP para um editor
5. [ ] Expandir para outros editores via adapters

# Cross-Platform IDE Plugin Development - Discovery Document

> Research conducted: January 2026
> Topic: Building plugins that work across multiple IDEs (VSCode, JetBrains, Zed)

## Executive Summary

**There is no unified framework like "React Native for IDE plugins"** that allows writing a single codebase deployable to all editors. However, **standardized protocols** exist that enable sharing 60-80% of core logic across editors through thin adapter layers.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Cross-Platform Protocols](#cross-platform-protocols)
2. [Editor-Specific Architectures](#editor-specific-architectures)
3. [Agent Client Protocol (ACP) - Deep Dive](#agent-client-protocol-acp---deep-dive)
4. [Recommended Architecture](#recommended-architecture)
5. [Comparison Matrix](#comparison-matrix)
6. [Resources & Links](#resources--links)

---

## Cross-Platform Protocols

### 1. Language Server Protocol (LSP) - The Gold Standard

**The de facto standard for cross-editor language features.**

| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Created by | Microsoft |
| Current Version | 3.17 |
| Transport | JSON-RPC 2.0 |
| Support | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim, Emacs, Sublime, Eclipse, Visual Studio |

#### Features Covered by LSP

- **Code Intelligence**
  - Autocomplete / IntelliSense
  - Go to Definition / Declaration / Implementation
  - Find All References
  - Hover documentation
  - Signature Help

- **Diagnostics**
  - Errors, warnings, hints
  - Real-time validation

- **Code Manipulation**
  - Code Actions / Quick Fixes
  - Rename / Refactor
  - Formatting (document & range)
  - Organize imports

- **Navigation**
  - Document symbols
  - Workspace symbols
  - Folding ranges
  - Breadcrumbs

#### LSP Architecture

```
┌─────────────────┐                      ┌──────────────────┐
│   VSCode        │◄────── JSON-RPC ────►│                  │
├─────────────────┤                      │                  │
│   JetBrains     │◄────── JSON-RPC ────►│  Language Server │
├─────────────────┤                      │   (your code)    │
│   Zed           │◄────── JSON-RPC ────►│                  │
├─────────────────┤                      │                  │
│   Neovim        │◄────── JSON-RPC ────►│                  │
└─────────────────┘                      └──────────────────┘
```

#### Key Insight: LSP Does NOT Handle UI

LSP is purely a data protocol. It tells the editor "there's an error on line 42" but the editor decides how to render it (red underline, icon, tooltip style, etc.).

#### Implementation Languages

Language Servers can be written in any language:
- **TypeScript/JavaScript** - Most common, great tooling
- **Rust** - High performance (rust-analyzer, etc.)
- **Go** - gopls
- **Python** - pylsp
- **C#** - OmniSharp

---

### 2. Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP)

**The LSP equivalent for debugging.**

| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Created by | Microsoft |
| Transport | JSON-RPC |
| Support | VSCode, JetBrains (via LSP4IJ), Zed, Neovim, Eclipse |

#### Features Covered

- Breakpoints (line, conditional, function, data)
- Step execution (into, over, out)
- Variable inspection
- Call stack navigation
- Watch expressions
- Debug console / REPL

#### Architecture

```
┌─────────────┐     DAP      ┌─────────────────┐     Native     ┌──────────┐
│   Editor    │◄────────────►│  Debug Adapter  │◄──────────────►│ Debugger │
└─────────────┘              └─────────────────┘                └──────────┘
```

---

### 3. Model Context Protocol (MCP) - AI Integration

**Standard for AI tools to integrate with editors and access context.**

| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Created by | Anthropic (November 2024) |
| Adopted by | OpenAI (March 2025), Zed, IBM BeeAI, Microsoft Copilot Studio, Windsurf |
| Governance | Donated to Linux Foundation (December 2025) via AAIF |
| Transport | JSON-RPC 2.0 (LSP-inspired) |

#### Features Covered

- **Resources** - File access, project context
- **Tools** - Function execution, code manipulation
- **Prompts** - Custom prompt templates
- **Sampling** - LLM interaction patterns

#### MCP Does NOT Handle UI

Like LSP, MCP is a data/action protocol. The editor/client decides how to present information to users.

---

### 4. Agent Client Protocol (ACP) - Emerging Standard

**Protocol for AI coding agents to work across multiple IDEs.**

| Attribute | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| Created by | Zed + JetBrains (collaboration, October 2025) |
| Status | Active development |
| Current Support | Zed (native), Neovim (plugins), Marimo, JetBrains (coming soon) |
| VSCode | Uncertain (under discussion by Microsoft) |

#### Key Differentiator: ACP Has UI Semantics

Unlike LSP/MCP, ACP defines **UI interaction patterns** for coding agents:

| UI Feature | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| Multi-file diffs | Standard way to present changes across files |
| Live progress | Streaming updates as agent works |
| Agent following | Editor follows agent through codebase (shows what's being read/edited) |
| Rich Markdown | All human-readable text defaults to Markdown |
| Approval workflows | Standard patterns for approve/reject actions |

#### However: Rendering is Still Editor-Specific

ACP defines "what" to show, not "how" to render it:
```
ACP: "Show a 3-file diff for user approval"
Editor: Decides layout (side-by-side, inline), colors, fonts, etc.
```

#### Compatible Agents

- Claude Code
- Codex CLI
- Gemini
- Goose (by Block)
- StackPack

---

## Editor-Specific Architectures

### JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ Platform)

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| Languages | Kotlin (preferred), Java |
| Build System | Gradle + IntelliJ Platform Gradle Plugin 2.x |
| Runtime | JVM |
| Distribution | JetBrains Marketplace |

#### 2025.3 Changes

- IntelliJ IDEA Community and Ultimate unified into single distribution
- Use `intellijIdea()` dependency helper instead of `intellijIdeaCommunity()`
- IC builds no longer available for 2025.3+
- Plugin DevKit enhanced with Kotlin Notebook integration

#### Using LSP in JetBrains

Two approaches:
1. **LSP4IJ** - Open source LSP client by Red Hat (recommended)
2. **Native API** - Use IntelliJ Platform's built-in LSP support (limited)

#### Key APIs

- **PSI** (Program Structure Interface) - AST representation
- **UAST** (Unified AST) - Cross-language abstraction for JVM languages
- **VFS** (Virtual File System) - File access abstraction
- **Actions** - Commands and menu items
- **Tool Windows** - Custom panels

---

### VSCode

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| Languages | TypeScript, JavaScript |
| Build System | npm/yarn + webpack/esbuild |
| Runtime | Node.js (extension host process) |
| Distribution | VSCode Marketplace, Open VSX |

#### Extension Capabilities

- **Language Features** - Native LSP client support
- **Webviews** - Custom HTML/CSS/JS panels (sandboxed iframes)
- **Tree Views** - Sidebar panels with tree structures
- **Commands** - Keyboard shortcuts, command palette
- **Debug Adapters** - Native DAP support
- **Tasks** - Build/run configurations

#### Important Note (2025)

The Webview UI Toolkit for VS Code was deprecated January 1, 2025. Extensions needing custom UI should use standard web technologies or consider alternatives.

---

### Zed

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| Languages | Rust (compiled to WebAssembly) |
| Build System | Cargo + wasm32-wasip1 target |
| Runtime | Wasmtime (sandboxed) |
| Distribution | Zed Extension Registry |

#### Why WebAssembly?

- Cross-platform without recompilation
- Sandboxed execution (can't crash editor)
- Fast execution
- Single artifact for all platforms

#### Current Capabilities

- Language support (via Tree-sitter + LSP)
- Themes and icon themes
- Slash commands
- MCP servers
- Debugger protocols (DAP)

#### Current Limitations

- No custom UI panels
- No arbitrary HTTP requests
- Limited filesystem access
- No modification of editor chrome

---

## Agent Client Protocol (ACP) - Deep Dive

### Why ACP Matters

Before ACP, agent developers had to:
1. Build VSCode extension
2. Build JetBrains plugin
3. Build Zed extension
4. Build Neovim plugin
5. Or rely on CLI-only interface

ACP provides a **universal integration point** - implement ACP once, work in all compatible editors.

### Protocol Flow

```
┌─────────────────┐                         ┌─────────────────┐
│                 │  1. Initialize          │                 │
│     Editor      │────────────────────────►│     Agent       │
│   (ACP Client)  │                         │  (ACP Server)   │
│                 │  2. Capabilities        │                 │
│                 │◄────────────────────────│                 │
│                 │                         │                 │
│                 │  3. Context/Files       │                 │
│                 │────────────────────────►│                 │
│                 │                         │                 │
│                 │  4. Proposed Changes    │                 │
│                 │◄────────────────────────│                 │
│                 │                         │                 │
│                 │  5. User Approval       │                 │
│                 │────────────────────────►│                 │
│                 │                         │                 │
│                 │  6. Apply Changes       │                 │
│                 │◄────────────────────────│                 │
└─────────────────┘                         └─────────────────┘
```

### Key Design Principles

1. **Editor-Agnostic Agents** - Agents work anywhere ACP is supported
2. **No Vendor Lock-in** - Switch agents without switching editors
3. **Confidence & Control** - Users see exactly what agents plan to do
4. **Great UX** - Streaming updates, clear intent, native feel

---

## Recommended Architecture

### Multi-Layer Strategy

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       Core Logic Layer                          │
│              (TypeScript or Rust - business logic)              │
│                                                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐ │
│  │   LSP Server    │  │   DAP Server    │  │   MCP Server    │ │
│  │  (lang features)│  │   (debugging)   │  │  (AI context)   │ │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘ │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                              │
             ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
             ▼                ▼                ▼
    ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
    │    VSCode    │  │   JetBrains  │  │     Zed      │
    │   Extension  │  │    Plugin    │  │   Extension  │
    │              │  │              │  │              │
    │  - Spawns    │  │  - Uses      │  │  - WASM      │
    │    servers   │  │    LSP4IJ    │  │    wrapper   │
    │  - UI config │  │  - UI config │  │  - Config    │
    └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
```

### Code Sharing Breakdown

| Layer | Shared | Editor-Specific |
|-------|--------|-----------------|
| Language Logic | 100% | 0% |
| LSP/DAP Servers | 100% | 0% |
| Server Spawning | 0% | 100% |
| Configuration UI | 0% | 100% |
| Custom Panels | 0% | 100% |
| Commands/Actions | ~50% | ~50% |

**Realistic code sharing: 60-80%** when using LSP/DAP/MCP properly.

---

## Comparison Matrix

### Cross-Platform Frameworks vs IDE Plugins

| Aspect | React Native / Tauri | IDE Plugins |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| Model | Unified framework | Protocols + adapters |
| Code Sharing | 90%+ | 60-80% |
| UI | Component abstraction | Editor-specific |
| Build | Single toolchain | Multiple toolchains |
| Deployment | One artifact | Per-editor artifacts |
| Learning Curve | Learn framework once | Learn each ecosystem |

### Why No "React Native for IDEs" Exists

1. **Vastly Different UI APIs** - Each editor has unique UI paradigms
2. **Deep Integration Needs** - Plugins need access to editor internals
3. **Established Ecosystems** - Marketplaces, communities already exist
4. **Security Models Vary** - Sandboxing, permissions differ greatly
5. **Performance Requirements** - Editors are performance-critical

---

## Resources & Links

### Official Protocol Documentation

| Protocol | Official Site | Specification |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| LSP | [microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/) | [3.17 Spec](https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/specifications/lsp/3.17/specification/) |
| DAP | [microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol](https://microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol/) | [Spec](https://microsoft.github.io/debug-adapter-protocol/specification) |
| MCP | [modelcontextprotocol.io](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) | [2025-11-25](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25) |
| ACP | [zed.dev/acp](https://zed.dev/acp) | [GitHub](https://github.com/agentclientprotocol/agent-client-protocol) |

### Editor Development Documentation

| Editor | Plugin SDK | Quick Start |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| JetBrains | [IntelliJ Platform SDK](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/developing-plugins.html) | [Quick Start](https://plugins.jetbrains.com/docs/intellij/plugins-quick-start.html) |
| VSCode | [Extension API](https://code.visualstudio.com/api) | [Your First Extension](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/get-started/your-first-extension) |
| Zed | [Extension Docs](https://zed.dev/docs/extensions/developing-extensions) | [Extensions Blog](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions) |

### Key Libraries & Tools

| Tool | Purpose | Link |
|------|---------|------|
| LSP4IJ | LSP client for JetBrains | [GitHub](https://github.com/redhat-developer/lsp4ij) |
| vscode-languageclient | LSP client for VSCode | [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/vscode-languageclient) |
| tower-lsp | LSP server framework (Rust) | [crates.io](https://crates.io/crates/tower-lsp) |
| vscode-languageserver | LSP server framework (TS) | [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/package/vscode-languageserver) |

### Announcements & Blog Posts

- [JetBrains × Zed: ACP Announcement](https://blog.jetbrains.com/ai/2025/10/jetbrains-zed-open-interoperability-for-ai-coding-agents-in-your-ide/)
- [IntelliJ Platform 2025.3 Changes](https://blog.jetbrains.com/platform/2025/11/intellij-platform-2025-3-what-plugin-developers-should-know/)
- [Zed Extensions Deep Dive](https://zed.dev/blog/zed-decoded-extensions)
- [Anthropic MCP Introduction](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol)
- [ACP Introduction by Block/Goose](https://block.github.io/goose/blog/2025/10/24/intro-to-agent-client-protocol-acp/)

---

## Conclusion

### Decision Framework

| If you need... | Use... |
|----------------|--------|
| Language features (autocomplete, diagnostics) | **LSP** |
| Debugging | **DAP** |
| AI tool integration | **MCP** |
| AI agent integration with UI | **ACP** |
| Custom UI panels | Editor-specific APIs |

### Recommended Approach

1. **Identify core functionality** - What does your plugin do?
2. **Map to protocols** - Which protocols cover your needs?
3. **Choose implementation language** - TypeScript (broad support) or Rust (performance + Zed native)
4. **Build core servers** - LSP, DAP, MCP as needed
5. **Create thin adapters** - Minimal editor-specific code
6. **Iterate** - Start with one editor, expand to others

The investment in protocol-based architecture pays off significantly when targeting multiple editors.

---
